text: Extremely short-duration events (≤30 seconds) may benefit from an explosive all-out pacing strategy.
During prolonged events lasting more than 2 minutes, performance times may be improved if athletes distribute their pace more evenly rather than using an all-out strategy.
During middle-distance (1.5-2 minutes) and ultra-endurance (>4 hours) events, well-trained athletes tend to adopt a positive pacing strategy, progressively slowing after reaching peak speed.
Six distinct pacing strategies have been identified in athletic competition: negative, all-out, positive, even, parabolic-shaped, and variable.
Self-selected exercise intensity is believed to be regulated by the brain based on peripheral sensory feedback and the anticipated workload remaining.
The rate and capacity limitations of anaerobic and aerobic energy supply are particularly influential in dictating the optimal pacing strategy during exercise.
